item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why wasn't Dr. Thaddeus Xyank, or his Temporal Anomalies Department, contacted while this is all going on? He was just as anomalous as SCP-711-EX. They can just send him back to the point where he disappeared, and with the Ethics Committee existing, would be able to approve this procedure.

Is this SCP taking place in one of those Alternate Foundations where the Temporal Anomalies Department is considered a hostile anomaly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd argue it would fit better as a neutralized scip, as the underlying anomaly that brought about Euclid's temporal displacement remains unknown (and hence not explained).

However, from the document's perspective, Euclid hasn't been neutralized yet, which explains why he is still classified as just a normal scip.

I really like the way it was written, characters felt real while still following the interview format and it's a neat concept overall, a bit refreshing how this article actually makes the humanoid SCP feel human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The footnote thing is a stylistic choice. I always find it weird to finish an article and then have an awkward footnote block at the end. And the “himself” part is intentional; it’s supposed to humanize Euclid and show that whoever is writing that part is treating him like an actual person.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your footnote is above the addendums and in your Description you said "Refers to himself", should probably be "Refers to itself". Besides that good concept +1 gamer

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
+ Response
- collapse

On the bit about the prime numbers, you do make a good point about it being unwieldy. I suppose I can change it to just him repeating the first four prime numbers, since those are about as much as can be easily recognized. And Euclid spent quite a bit of time on prime numbers, even being credited with the proof of infinite primes by multiplying a set of primes and adding one to find a new prime (or product of new primes). That's also why it's just a suspicion, and partly why the researcher asks Euclid who he is instead of asking if he is in fact Euclid.

Part of the reason I wrote Euclid to be so interested in mathematics is that, besides the fact that Euclid did dedicate a significant amount of his time to learning, discovering, and writing about math, it would also be something that he would want to see where it went. Learning that his results were expanded and improved upon, such as how the converse of a theorem he proved was later also proven 1,000 years after him, would definitely have been interesting for him. And there's also the fact that we don't have a lot of information about Euclid's personal life, besides his math writings.

I see what you're saying about the ending not having any buildup. I originally strayed away from having a transcript of the O5 discussion included in the article, but I suppose that would be a good way to actually attach weight to the decision. In my mind, the fact that Euclid did end up being released was fairly monumental, at least in the sense of anomalous human rights. I viewed it as a demonstration of the conflict between parts of the O5 that won't neutralize an anomaly at all if they can help it and parts that view the containment of anomalous humans that could safely and easily have their properties nullified as unethical. I can completely understand how this wasn't well communicated, however, especially since I reduced this conflict and resolution into one paragraph at the end.

Anyways, thanks again for critiquing this article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would be nice to see an interview with him telling some interesting facts about Ancient Greek era, e.g. about some ancient anomalies.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
I've chosen to give this a review as a member of the Site Criticism Team since it's been middling between negative and positive rating for a little while.

+[Critique] Click to Access
-[Click to Hide Text]

Not much to comment on in regards to the containment procedures here. Concise, leaves enough to the imagination. Nothing too unusual.

The identity of SCP-4721 was suspected after Dr. Krain, the first researcher to initiate contact, recognized that the entity was attempting to communicate through tapping the sequence of prime numbers.

That's a rather specific thing to notice. If it were me, I wouldn't know it from any other kind of arrhythmic tapping, especially since it gets a little hazy after thirteen. Even then, just about anybody could be doing this. It's a bit too coincidental on top of a huge leap of logic.

This interview is kind of fun. I'm not sure if you needed an interview to get this across, but it does give us a bit of personality and conflict with 4721. I feel that there is more to 4721's interests than mathematics, though. I mean, it's been two thousand years. Surely he had other interests than math?

So, we're seeing the conflict realized in this second interview. I'm still a bit baffled that Euclid could have dedicated his entire life and desires to mathematics. It just barely breaks my suspension of disbelief. But I get it. He's the founder. Of course he'd be this invested and frustrated by the advances made.

However, I'm a little let-down by the end of the article. It seems like so easy of a solution, all without a build-up that has any real consequences. So what if Euclid is frustrated by his inability of advanced mathematics? Why should the Foundation care? I guess that's brought out by the O5 vote and the Ethics vote, but it just doesn't feel like it's rewarding to me.

All-in-all, I'm not finding myself to be entertained by this. The entity is only anomalous by their nature of being from a different time, and the acclimation into the modern era is unsuccessful, so much so that they just give in and amnesticize him. I don't feel like anything was particularly lost or gained here.

This is a downvote from me. There isn't anything I find remarkable about this piece.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to RockTeethMothEyes or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bizarre and not entirely "anomalous", but I do enjoy the use of Euclid - someone had to do it.
Upvote for me cause I see potential

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The bit where Euclid is referred to both as “the entity” and as “he/him/himself” is partially intentional. The parts referencing him as a person are supposed to be written by Dr. Krain, such as the note after the first interview and the last paragraph of the document, while the sections that would not be written by Dr. Krain, such as the containment procedures, use the term “entity”. That was a halfterthought though, so I could make it more obvious by more clearly sectioning it, such as making the description either fully use “the entity” or fully use pronouns.

And I suppose explicitly stating Euclid is a male would be better than just implying it with “himself”, so I’ll probably change “human” to “male human”, since that’s a bit more professional than just “man”. Thanks for your advice!

Edit: Yeah, I’ve gotten similar feedback about how the scip could potentially just be a normal person from our time. I put in that statement about “temporal analysis”, but I suppose that is pretty vague and doesn’t really help support the idea of Euclid being from the distant past. I had an idea that this “analysis” was something along the lines of how we can differentiate between steel made after the first nuclear weapons test and steel made before (including steel from ships that sunk before these tests), as the metal made after has small traces of radiation. While that doesn’t really apply in this scenario, I did just think of something that could be used in place of it, so I might add that in soon.

And you are completely right about why I didn’t elaborate on the time travel. Although I suppose it might be beneficial to add in a blurb about how the Foundation has yet to find an explanation for Euclid’s temporal displacement. Anyways, thanks again for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I will read it when I have spare time since it looks somewhat promising yet struggling to survive on the site.

At this moment I have one little advice: call it a man, not a human, if you are going to mention it him by the word "himself" in the same sentence you're introducing him.

It does not matter that much, but on this site, I think, it is fine to describe the person as a person, not a thing. So there is no need to call him an entity etc. The author chooses one — basically what I mean.

25 Jun 2019
To be honest, I'm mildly disappointed by SCP-4721's description of the main anomaly. It's just… there. Time travel with no events. But I can understand that, it's hard to expand at this phenomena without harming the idea to show how close he is to being released and how reasonable it would be.

But the story is more about how hard it is to climb the mountain you've used to climb before when its top is now always there to block the sun. +1 as a token of appreciation of competent writing.

Still, what we have here is just one normal person that might have come from the past, but there is no strong indication of that. The article goes through claims as if they're proofs of the anomaly. I'm not feeling like I'm reading an SCP, that might be the case for dudes who downvote. Just sharing my analysis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, it's not just the memory wipe but also the fact that he gets released back into society that's better than him being stuck in containment. And I don't really see how that would be worse for him mentally, especially since the Foundation will help him reintegrate back into society, not just abandon him on the street. This can't be the first time they've had to fully wipe someone and make sure they have a somewhat-normal life afterwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Surely completely wiping the dude's memory - enough to completely erase his past life such that he can integrate with modern day without noticing anything is off - be far, far worse for him mentally than the anachronism in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Easy +1 for me. Thanks a ton for writing! It takes a lot to build a character in so few words and you've done it masterfully. Barring that the Foundation seems to want him to be happy on some level, it's the same despair and yearning for purpose I try to capture in my Humanoid articles.

Except, you know, my SCP-4051 took 12k words to do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Response
- collapse

Your first question is actually something that’s still being debated even today. I’m personally in the “math is discovered” camp rather than “math is invented”, with the caveat that you can invent explanations or proofs of mathematics, just not the actual underlying mathematical truths themselves.

As for the second bit, yeah I suppose that does work better. I’ll make the edit, thanks!

And I see where you’re coming from for the third part. I’ll change it a bit so it’s more professional than “I have an idea”.

I originally did plan on adding an interview immediately after his initial containment, but then realized I had no idea how to write a dialogue that occurred in Ancient Greek between a native speaker and a non-native one that was then translated into English. Plus, I was worried I wouldn’t be able to fully explain everything well, as I figured Euclid would have a lot of questions, considering he just got thrown over 2000 years into the future. I do agree though that Euclid would benefit from some more character development, so I’ll think about some ways I could do that.

Thanks for your critique!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Couple of potential nitpicks
- Close

all the mathematics that’s been discovered since your time

Is "discovered" the correct phrase? Perhaps something like "all the progress in mathematics that has occurred" or something similar would be more appropriate.

Did you want to talk about it?

Would "do" be more appropriate than "did"? "Did" seems to imply that Euclid asked for the interview.

Actually, I have an idea.

To me, this seems a bit unprofessional. Maybe something like "We may actually be able to help you" would be better. This might just be personal preference.

I feel the idea behind this article is quite interesting and, even in its current form, I do like the article enough to upvote.

However, I also feel that currently, the article is perhaps a bit "bare-bones", which causes it to feel somewhat rushed and diminishes the potential impact of the story you are trying to tell. Perhaps you could add another interview ahead of the current one, set directly after the discovery of Euclid. This could allow you to establish Euclid better as a character, which could increase the impact of the second interview.

These are, of course, just my thoughts and more experienced users might be able to go more in-depth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This is for Magnus' stupid dumbhead contest"

A slightly-rushed entry for oldcon 2019, but I at least like that stance I took on it. Could probably use some tightening up in a few places, so feel free to point anything out and I'll see about improving it. Thanks for reading!

Edit: Added a new interview log, which should hopefully flesh out the piece a bit. Feel free to point out any places it could use some editing!

Thanks again to LittleFieryOne, AKFrost, Kakroom, Oboebandgeek99, and Waterfire for critique!
